title: Liege waffles
subtitle: Belgian enriched yeast dough waffles with pearl sugar
excerpt: Liege waffles are made from a brioche-like yeast dough, not a poured batter. Pearl sugar is folded into the proved dough and caramelises against the hot iron, forming a crunchy, lacquered crust. The waffles are dense and chewy with pockets of amber caramel sugar throughout.
sourceNotes: The Liege waffle (gaufre de Liege) originates from the Belgian city of Liege and is attributed to an eighteenth-century court recipe. Unlike Brussels waffles, which are batter-based, the Liege waffle uses a yeast-leavened dough enriched with butter and eggs. The defining feature is Belgian pearl sugar (nib sugar, hagelzucker), which caramelises during cooking rather than dissolving. The recipe follows the established Belgian street-food tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely. Freeze individually wrapped. Reheat in a hot dry pan or back in the waffle iron for 2 to 3 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be proved overnight in the fridge after the first rise. The next day, knock back, fold in the pearl sugar, and cook straight from the fridge without a second prove.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked in a waffle iron; temperature cannot be specified as it depends on the iron

glossaryTerms.definition: Pearl sugar must be folded into the dough only after the first prove, not during mixing. Added too early, the yeast activity and gluten development crush the sugar before baking. Fold in gently using the dough hook on low speed or by hand, ensuring the pieces distribute without being broken down. Some cracking of the sugar pieces is unavoidable and acceptable.

content.text: Liege waffles taste nothing like the batter-based kind. They are closer to an enriched bread than a pancake. The
matters: fold it in after the first prove. The waffle iron does not need to be greased; the butter in the dough and the caramelising sugar prevent sticking.

Combine 500 g flour, 7 g yeast, 30 g sugar, and 8 g salt in the bowl of a stand mixer fitted with the dough hook. Add 3 eggs, 120 ml warm milk, and 1 tsp vanilla. Mix on low for 2 minutes to combine. Increase to medium and add 200 g softened butter a few cubes at a time, waiting for each addition to be absorbed before adding the next. Mix for 8 to 10 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky. It will be quite soft. Cover and prove at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.
Add the pearl sugar and cook

Knock back the proved dough. Add 200 g pearl sugar and fold in gently on low speed or by hand until the sugar is evenly distributed without being crushed. Divide into 8 equal pieces of about 110 g each. Roll into balls and rest for 20 to 30 minutes. Heat the waffle iron to its highest setting. Cook each dough ball for 3 to 4 minutes, closing the lid firmly, until the waffles are deep golden with caramelised patches of amber sugar. They will look darker than you expect; the sugar caramelises heavily. Eat warm.

items.problem: Waffle iron sticks even though the dough is buttery
items.solution: The iron was not hot enough when the dough went in. Preheat the iron fully and give it an extra minute before the first waffle. The early caramelisation releases the dough from the plates.

items.problem: Pearl sugar disappears rather than caramelising
items.solution: Regular granulated sugar was used instead of pearl sugar. Pearl sugar is specifically formulated to resist melting and hold its structure during baking. Granulated sugar will dissolve into the dough entirely. Pearl sugar is available in supermarket baking aisles and online.
